speaker: interviewer
text: 1996. Mr. Walsh, was Male Werbcze modernized? Were the streets paved?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was no paving, with just the regular road dirt road and no sidewalks. No sewers or anything else-- it was just a--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did-- how was the house lit? What about plumbing?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had no plumbing. We have-- we had two outhouse in the back of the house. In the winter, you had to go in the back of the house. Fortunately, we had, like I said, a big barn. So we could-- for the kids, we could run out in the winter into the barn. And there was some hay that you could take care of and then throw it out. But it was an outhouse. And there were-- lights were kerosene lamps. And we had a stove in the middle of the house made of brick. And this is how the house was heated. And another stove in the kitchen, which was for cooking, like one of those old-fashioned stoves

speaker: interviewer
text: To the best of your memory, how long did this-- did life go on like this, until what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we-- our-- we were lucky when the war in 1939 broke out. So we were on the western-- on the eastern part of Ukraine. Actually, it's the western, but east of Poland. And Germany occupied Poland. And we were taken over by the Soviet Union. And we lived like this, I would say, considering the times and everything else, fairly good until the Germans came in, in 19-- in August 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you say the Russians took over in 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1939, The Russians took over--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did--

speaker: interviewee
text: --until 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they-- how did they take over? Did they actually march in?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians marched in. And there was skirmishes between the Russians, and the Polish army, and so on. But later on, the Polish retreated and they were fighting the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you actually had German-- Russian soldiers in your village?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. We had the Russian soldiers around us. And I would say, considering after the war, after Poland, it was-- the life was for us better in the Soviet Union than it was under the Polish regime before. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: --we were-- well, we were already more grown up later on. And there was work. The Russians established a kolkhoz, a collective farm. And my brothers were working in there. And-- and more people were working, so we had a better life.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it affect you personally between 1939 and 1942?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well in 1939 till 1942, actually, I went to school and everything was normal. We had no-- no problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there ever any incidents with these Russian soldiers and you or your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they were all nice and very good to us. And some of them were friends with my eldest brother, Shabtai. And they were later on coming to our house. And we had no problems whatsoever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you speak Russian?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we later on started to learn. Actually, when the Russians came in, we learned Russian. So we started to speak Russian a bit. And I had no problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know what was going on in 1939 in the-- throughout Europe all around you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew from some newspapers. There was no radio but we had a neighbor that had some kind of a ham radio. I don't know how he got without electricity. But he did report to us about what's going on in Poland. And we also had some refugees that escaped from Poland to the Ukraine. And some of them stayed with us for a few months. And then they went, later on, deep into Russia. But we didn't know the atrocities that were really taking place as much as what we found out later on in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: So life went on fairly normally until 1942. Was your father able to continue being a hazzan?

speaker: interviewer
text: Up until 19-- I mean between, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, until 1942, oh yes. He was going to his work. And we had the farm. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was he a hazzan?

speaker: interviewee
text: He went to pray sometimes in the villages, sometimes to the town of Rafalowka-- which was a bigger town, and he was better paid-- and sometimes even with one of the big hazzanim in Stepan, which was called a big shul. And there was a hazzan by the name of [? Lever. ?] And it was a big honor at that time, I remember as a kid, that he was davening with hazzan [? Lever ?] at that time in the Stepan shul.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father make a good living from being a hazzan?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not really. He didn't make a good living out of it. But the supplement from the farm-- we could just survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had what to eat. All the children had--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we always had food, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And clothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened in 1942?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, actually, it started in June 1941 when the Germans, I think, bombed Kiev. It was actually June the 22nd, in 1941. They bombed Kiev, which was only a few hours away, but we could hear the bombing going on. And at that time, I remember it registered in my mind because my sister Sonia was getting engaged to a young man from one of the villages. And as the party was going on-- there wasn't a big crowd-- my grandparents, and some other family, and neighbors-- somebody-- a neighbor passed by. He came from the city and knocked, opened the door and say, listen people, there is a war. And we heard the bombing. But we didn't know what was going on. The Germans have attacked the Soviet Union. At this moment, my father knew what a war meant because he went through the First World War. He said to the young man, listen, we will call off the engagement because I'm sure they will take you to the army now. And who knows what's going to happen, so we'll just leave it as it is for now. And hopefully the war will finish soon and you will come back and we'll continue with that. And that's the last we have seen of that young man.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't remember what was his name now. Sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was in '41.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened between then and 1942?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Germans-- after-- the Germans came to us, the front-- the first front came in August 1942-- in August '41, 1941. I'm sorry. In August 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: They came into your village?

speaker: interviewee
text: They came into the village. They told us to move out of the house. And they took over our house. There was a big bus. And they stayed in our house. They were raping some of the women. My sister was hidden in the attic. And they were not too bad to us. They were quite, you know, I would say, human at that time. I even one time went for a ride with a German soldier on a motorcycle. And he was nice enough. And when I came back, my father was so angry that I thought that he would kill me or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said your sister was hidden in an attic. Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. In our house, in the attic, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the Germans were in there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Were in the house, but no, the attic was completely separate. You couldn't get in from the inside of house. The attic were like-- you had to come in only from the stall. And in the stall there was a cow. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: So just your sister was hidden?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister was hidden there, yeah. And my brothers were all going out to work because of that factory that my uncle had. So we all helped out, because the Germans wanted us to take care of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you stay while the Germans were in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the neighbors'. With the neighbors, some of us slept in a barn-- you know, in the barn. They didn't care where we stayed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your neighbor's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Patrikei.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was the same one--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --whose daughter you played with--

speaker: interviewee
text: That helped us.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and who helped you before?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah, nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was very good to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they were all good to us-- yeah, very good to us. Then, while the Germans were staying in our house, a man came up to my-- a German soldier-- or I don't know what his rank was-- came up to my father. And he said, I am Jewish. And he said, you better try to escape somewhere, because in Germany and in Poland, they're killing the Jews. And my father thought he was playing some kind of a trick, being a German soldier. And he was all alone in the house. He came in. And my father said, well, he didn't want to talk to him or anything else. He says, you don't believe me that I am Jewish. So he says, give me a siddur. The German soldier took a siddur and started to daven-- real daven, like a religious Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father didn't want to question him who, was, what, but just said-- he said, I just want to tell you. He was afraid. So he took maybe a few minutes to talk to my father. He says, if you can escape somewhere, to Russia or somewhere, escape, because the Germans will kill you. And then he just disappeared. So my father didn't take this seriously. He did hear about it from people telling about what was going on in Poland and the rest of the world. But he didn't believe in it, because he said, when the Germans came in 1918, during the First World War, they were such civilized people. And I don't think they would do what these people say. And he didn't believe it.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I forgot to mention that just before the Germans arrived, maybe a couple days before they arrived to our village, a friend of my brother Shabtai, who was in the Communist party, came and says, we are going deep into Russia. We're retreating. Please go with us. Take the whole family and go with us, because the Germans will kill you here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my eldest brother was begging my father. And my father-- well, we work so hard. We have still young kids. And where will we go? And he was scared. And he just didn't believe. And this is how we stayed. But a lot of people did escape to Russia. And some of them survived. So how long did this go on? You said this happened in around-- after June of '40--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is in August, 1941. Then this-- Germans right away went east, attacking the Soviet armies. And a skirmish started. The Germans came and confiscated clothing for the German army-- my father's coat, any warm coats that we had, because winter was coming, 1942. They took my father's coat. They took my mother's coats, anything warm. They came in. They didn't ask any questions. They just looked through cupboard. They took away our horse. They said they need it for the war. And they took away the cow. Then, sometimes in November 1941, there was a band of Ukrainian bandits came from another village and came to rob us. And they did take away a lot of our contents. And they beat up my brother Yitshak, who was talking to them and trying to say, look, you're not touching us or anything else. But they took everything away. Then the next day, my brother went to a Polish person that used to be-- that became a commandant-- what are called the police chief of the village. And he was very good to us. And we-- we were really-- my father was friends with them. And they came and they found those bandits, the Ukrainian in the village. And they took all the contents that they robbed us-- took away and brought it back. And from then on, they really were nice to us and took care of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So by this time, were you back in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were back in our house, yes. We lived in our house.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long-- how long were you kicked out of the house for by the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only for a couple weeks, for the first front line of the Germans that arrived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then they allowed you to go back in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then they allowed to back-- back into the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did they stay in, in Male Werbcze?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There were no Germans. They just--

speaker: interviewer
text: They just came in for two weeks?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had the police. They set up a German-- what do you call-- a run, a police of the Ukrainian Nazis, and all over. And they were running it-- like, as far as the Jews are concerned, they were rounding up the Jews, robbing. And now we heard they were already killing Jews and so on. But we were fortunate, like I said. Our neighbors and these people, the Boris Kovich, the commandant, or the chief of the police, took care of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So being a Polack.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he got your stuff back.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he got the stuff back. And they took care of us. And they were taking most of the Jews from all the other villages to the ghettos. And they left us until the last minute, they-- until they said that the Germans-- the commandant came and said, now I can't do anything about it. You will have to go. When they come to take you, you will have to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they came and they took us. In beginning of 1942 they came. And they-- my brother Yitshak stayed to work for the Germans in the factory and my uncle. And then my two brothers, like I said before, went to the labor camp in Kostopol. My mother, and I, and my sister, and my young second brother Yakov were in the ghetto until the August 1942. You were in what ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: We're in the Stepan ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Stepan. Now did you-- were you able to take anything with you to Stepan?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just very little, just some contents. Very little-- some small bags of sleeping blankets and cushions and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to take one of your favorite possessions?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't have any possessions, really, except some of my clothing or anything else. But I didn't-- I just went for the day when they took me. And then I escaped again. And I went to the Ukrainian. And I stayed at the Ukrainian, taking care of the kid, helping out. And like I said, I was taking care of him and bringing food to the ghetto while my parents were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your parents were in Stepan. How far away was Stepan from Male Werbcze?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nine kilometers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nine kilometers. So you're telling me that you-- you were all taken there, but then you escaped?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were staying with your neighbor. Did they come looking after you-- looking for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nobody came looking for me. Because actually children they didn't really count that much inside. I was, like I said, going in and out to the ghetto. At one time, I remember, where I was jumping over the fence and the police caught me. The Ukrainian guards caught me and they threw me into a cellar. And I was in the cellar for two days. And then my father came and somehow got me out of the cellar. And then I escaped again, until the day of the extermination.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did the ghetto look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was surrounded by a fence all around, barbed wire and a wood fence, and surrounded with guard-- German guards and Ukrainian guards. All inside, people lived like animals. Like in one room, there would be 10 people in a room. There was no food. There was a kitchen that they would serve you soup and some crackers once a day. That was a general kitchen. People were starving. There was-- there was disease. People were dying. And there was no medicine or doctors or anything else. But that was the way they wanted to do, the Germans to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you would sneak in and out of there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you able to do this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was dressed like a Ukrainian. Fortunately, at the time, I didn't look so Semitic. My hair were all shaven completely, because they were dark, black, and the Ukrainian don't look like this. But I had Ukrainian clothes on. And I was fast. And since I was a village boy brought up-- and I forgot to mention that we also belonged to-- when we were young, before the war, belonged to the Betar organization. And we had preparation for to fight back, not to give in. And we did fight back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the Jews in the ghetto-- did they have to wear a Jewish star?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. As a matter of fact, when we were-- as soon as the Germans came in, they-- all the-- all their Jews had to wear-- not a star. It was a yellow-- a yellow--

speaker: interviewer
text: A band?

speaker: interviewee
text: Insignia band in the front and in the back. And the kids had to wear a white band on their left hand with a Star of David on it. And I remember making it my own band and with a Star of David. And I somehow was proud of it, at that time, to wear it.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in Stepan?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Or in Male Werbcze?

speaker: interviewee
text: --in Male Werbcze and in Stepan. In the ghetto we had to wear all the time a band and a yellow-- they called it [NON-ENGLISH], or the yellow stripe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now how old were you when you were running back and forth to Stepan?

speaker: interviewee
text: 12 Years old. I was 12 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that feel as a 12-year-old boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know. It is a matter of survival. You don't think much. You just think on a daily basis.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what you felt? Did you feel fear? Did you feel anger? What do you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I don't remember even feeling fear, because maybe it was in me all the time. And I was angry, naturally, why. But I had no idea why they did this to us. Because somehow I grew up with the Ukrainian kids. And I felt I was the same as them, just because I was called Jewish and I had a different religion. But it didn't seem to me that I was that much different. But they made us-- when they take you into the ghetto, and de-humanize you, and so on, you really start believing maybe something wrong or something. But we didn't give in. And we were fighting back.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of food was it that you were bringing to your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, usually bread. Sometimes butter. Sometimes in the summer it was some vegetables or fruit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now the stuff you were bringing wasn't kosher.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we couldn't bring any meat or anything. But you know, if you have to survive, you're allowed to eat anything. But my father wouldn't eat meat, even though we could get from the neighbors. But we had some, like I said, butter and bread and vegetables and fruit. And that's how we survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did this go on that you were running back and forth with food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this was going on until the summer of 1942 until the liquidation started.

speaker: interviewer
text: So a few months.

speaker: interviewee
text: A few months, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened in the liquidation?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the liquidation, like I said, when I arrived to visit my parents, the liquidation started. And we were hiding in a attic, in one of rooms, in one of the houses where we stayed.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Stepan.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Stepan, in the ghetto. It was actually near the river name Horyn-- the name of the river, Horyn, in the Stepan, a big river, which goes through there. And my brother and my sister was with me, my mother, and my father. And when we heard the voices, there was crying and screaming. And it was very scary. My mother told me to jump. So I jumped from the window. It was very high up-- I don't know, like one floor. And I fell down. And then my mother jumped after me. And then I heard just noise inside the room. And the Germans-- and the Nazis took my brother, my sister, and my father to the extermination. And my mother and I run into this house, at the farmer's. And in the morning, he-- like I said before, he threw-- he chased us out. We were on our way through the village. And we asked the Ukrainian farmer where the village road is. And he took us right away to the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Police.

speaker: interviewee
text: --police station, where they were gathering the escapees for the extermination. And my mother begged him, but he wouldn't let us go. So my mother told me in Yiddish, [YIDDISH]. So I didn't want to leave her, but she says, [YIDDISH], and just begged me. If I survived she would be very happy. So I just fell down on the ground, turned, and ran away. And he took my mother and I run away into the village of Male Werbcze. And I was-- I went straight to my friends-- Joachim, which was-- the father was the mayor of the village. And that's how I escaped.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you in Male Werbcze this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed in Male Werbcze until beginning of 1943. The people in the village couldn't take care of us anymore. They were saying, look, we can't let you into the house. We can't give you any food. Because if we catch somebody-- if somebody will catch us that you-- we're helping you out or saving you, they will kill us and burn the house with it. And it-- there were cases where they did kill and they burned houses. And they insisted that we should go somewhere else. We were inquiring about where to go. And we found out there was a small-- a big village, actually, by the name of Huta Stepanska, where a Polish colony, Polacks, lived there. And they made their own, like, state. They were all armed. And they were defending themselves against the Ukrainian bandits that tried to kill them. There was a fight between Ukrainians and the Polish at that time. I presume that the Germans have equipped them both with arms so they should kill each other and not to touch them. And my brother Chaim said-- we had a meeting. Always we used to meet at night for dividing the food between us. Chaim-- my brother Chaim, Yakov, and Yitshak and myself. Shabtai was already at that time killed. And he decided he would go into the town of Huta Stepanska, find out if these people could help us maybe, and we could maybe hide there. He went in there and he found out that yeah, it's true, there are some Jews there and they don't touch them. And we could stay with them there or work for some of the Polish families. He came back. And the next night, we packed up. And about one o'clock in the morning we left. And it was only about 20 kilometers away from the village of Male Werbcze. And we arrived early in the morning to this Polish enclave, Huta Stepanska. The Polish police met us there. And they got all nervous. They thought that we were Ukrainian. And they took my brother Chaim. And they beat him up. They said he was a spy and he was giving information to the Ukrainian bandits. And we were begging. We said, we're Jews. They didn't believe us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they took us to a house somewhere. It looked like a police station. And they said we've got to kill you. We've got to shoot you. And my brothers were saying, why would you want to kill us? We-- we are Jews. He says, no, no. You are spies for the Ukrainian. Whether they were anti-Semites or what, I don't know. Maybe they really thought we're Ukrainians. And we started to speak Yiddish to them.

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't believe us. And they were just-- left us there in the field. And they said, we're going to kill you. And one guy was standing with a gun. And we were crying and begging. And we were all the three of us-- four of us were standing there and begging. And said, he's going to kill us any time. And we were talking between us, maybe we'll start to run, and maybe one of us will escape. And as we were thinking about to escape and run, a cavalryman-- a guy came on a horse and said something to the guard, and they let us go. It was like a [NON-ENGLISH] that came and they let us go. And we went to look to some families to find some work.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Huta Stepanska.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Huta Stepanska. My brothers did find work on a farm. We were all from a village, like I said, so we easy to find work. And I went into a Polish family there. And the woman took me in right away. She started questioning me. I told her I was Jewish. And she was very nice to me. She was a widow with a daughter and a son-in-law and a kid. And her husband was killed by the Ukrainian bandits. And we were staying there and working for them. We were meeting from time to time. And this was really a heaven, because we didn't have to hide, just work. And then, this was the end of just about the spring of 1943 or beginning of spring 1943, the Ukrainian bandits have organized an army of about maybe, I don't know, 8,000, 10,000 soldiers or bandits with arms, and attacked the town of Huta Stepanska in order to liquidate the Polish enclave and kill them all. And they did it before in some other small villages-- killed all the Polacks. And the Polacks-- the Polish people started to run away, to a town by the name of Rafalowka, which was called [? Nova ?] Rafalowka. They were all running away to the town of Rafalowka. And from there, they were going-- the Germans would take the young ones on the trains and ship them to Germany to work for them. We decided to go with the Polish people. And we came into the town of Rafalowka. We found some people that gave us some food. And we found some Jews in there that escaped. And there was a lady that was in Huta Stepanska, a young girl that came with us. She was all alone, so she attached herself to us. And she was with us in Rafalowka. In there, we went to the station where they were recruiting people, young people, to go to work in Germany. We were in the lineup. And as the people were coming out from the checkup and registration, we asked if they-- how do they check. And they said they undress everybody, presumably checking if somebody was Jewish, so they could check. Well, in that case, we decided we cannot go to Germany because they would undress us and find out we're Jews and we're finished.
